claims.claim_text: Kafka was built at LinkedIn in 2011 to connect hundreds of microservices without point-to-point integrations.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2 sentence 1
claims.anchor_text: LinkedIn built Kafka in 2011 to solve a specific problem: connecting hundreds of microservices without point-to-point spaghetti.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kafka was created to support large-scale real-time data pipelines without tight point-to-point coupling.
claims.rationale: The historical origin is citable, but the exact problem framing and scale detail are too specific for the sources located here.

claims.claim_text: Over 80% of Fortune 100 companies run Kafka.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2 sentence 2
claims.anchor_text: Today, over 80% of Fortune 100 companies run Kafka.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kafka is widely adopted across large enterprises.
claims.rationale: This is a drifting enterprise-adoption statistic and no allowlisted primary source was found that directly backs this exact number.

claims.claim_text: Netflix processes 7 million events per second through Kafka.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2 sentence 3
claims.anchor_text: Netflix processes 7 million events per second through it.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Large streaming platforms use Kafka for very high-volume event pipelines.
claims.rationale: The named-company throughput figure is precise but no primary source was found that directly supports this exact number.

claims.claim_text: The New York Times uses Kafka to deliver articles in real time.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2 sentence 4
claims.anchor_text: The New York Times uses it to deliver articles in real time.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Media platforms use Kafka to move content and event data in near real time.
claims.rationale: This named-user anecdote needs a direct source and none was established from the allowlisted or clearly identifiable primary sources available.

claims.claim_text: Uber routes every trip through Kafka.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 2 sentence 5
claims.anchor_text: Uber routes every trip through Kafka.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Ride-sharing platforms use Kafka as a core part of their real-time data platforms.
claims.rationale: Uber engineering sources confirm heavy Kafka usage, but not this exact every-trip formulation.

claims.claim_text: Strimzi is a CNCF-incubating project for running Apache Kafka on Kubernetes.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 4 sentence 1
claims.rationale: The Strimzi GitHub README directly states both its Kubernetes purpose and CNCF incubating status.

claims.claim_text: Strimzi manages Kafka declaratively through Kubernetes custom resources across the cluster lifecycle.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 4 sentence 2
claims.rationale: Official Strimzi documentation is the right primary source for this lifecycle-management claim, but `strimzi.io` is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Jay Kreps said the project was named after Franz Kafka because it was 'a system optimized for writing.'
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 1
claims.rationale: The cited history section on the Apache Kafka page directly covers the naming rationale and attribution to Jay Kreps.

claims.claim_text: A single Kafka broker can sustain 800 MB/s of throughput, about 2.8 TB per hour.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 2
claims.anchor_text: - **A single Kafka broker can sustain 800 MB/s of throughput** on appropriate hardware. That is roughly 2.8 TB per hour, per broker. Most performance problems are caused by misconfiguration, not Kafka's limits.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Kafka brokers can sustain very high throughput** on appropriate hardware, but the exact ceiling depends on workload, storage, and network characteristics.
claims.rationale: The exact throughput figure is benchmark-like and hardware-dependent, and no authoritative source was found for this precise number.

claims.claim_text: KRaft removes the ZooKeeper dependency by running metadata management inside Kafka with a Raft-based quorum, available in Kafka 3.3.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 3
claims.rationale: Apache Kafka's KRaft documentation for 3.3 directly describes KRaft roles and the metadata quorum.

claims.claim_text: Strimzi supports KRaft deployments.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 3 sentence 3
claims.rationale: Official Strimzi deployment docs are the appropriate primary source for current KRaft support status, but the domain is off-list.

claims.claim_text: ZooKeeper mode is deprecated for Apache Kafka.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know, bullet 3 sentence 3
claims.rationale: Apache Kafka's ZooKeeper operations page explicitly marks ZooKeeper as deprecated.

claims.claim_text: Within a consumer group, each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer, so partition count caps parallelism for a single group.
claims.span_hint: section: Why Partitions Matter, paragraph 1
claims.rationale: The KafkaConsumer docs directly explain that each partition is assigned to exactly one consumer within a consumer group.

claims.claim_text: Kafka guarantees ordering within a partition, but not across partitions.
claims.span_hint: section: Why Partitions Matter, paragraph 3
claims.rationale: The Kafka introduction explicitly distinguishes per-partition ordering from the lack of ordering across partitions.

claims.claim_text: If a producer specifies a key but no partition, Kafka chooses the partition using a hash of the key.
claims.span_hint: section: Why Partitions Matter, paragraph 3 sentence 3
claims.rationale: The ProducerRecord API docs directly state that a key without an explicit partition is hashed to choose a partition.

claims.claim_text: KRaft provides controller failover in seconds rather than minutes.
claims.span_hint: section: KRaft: Kafka Without ZooKeeper, advantages bullet 2
claims.rationale: The comparative timing is precise and benchmark-like, but no authoritative source was found that directly supports this exact seconds-versus-minutes claim.

claims.claim_text: KRaft scales to millions of partitions.
claims.span_hint: section: KRaft: Kafka Without ZooKeeper, advantages bullet 3
claims.rationale: The exact scale claim is a numeric performance assertion and no primary source was found that directly substantiates this phrasing.

claims.claim_text: Apicurio Registry is an open-source schema registry that can use Kafka as its storage backend.
claims.span_hint: section: Apicurio Registry on Kubernetes, intro sentence
claims.anchor_text: Apicurio Registry is the open-source schema registry that works with Kafka (alternative to Confluent Schema Registry):
claims.suggested_rewrite: Apicurio Registry is an open-source schema registry that can use Kafka as its storage backend:
claims.rationale: The GitHub repository supports the open-source and Kafka-backed parts, but not the broader comparative claim as written.

claims.claim_text: Kafka Connect is a framework for streaming data between Kafka and external systems.
claims.span_hint: section: Kafka Connect: Moving Data In and Out, intro sentence
claims.rationale: The official Kafka Connect overview directly describes Kafka Connect as a framework for moving data between Kafka and other systems.

claims.claim_text: The Debezium PostgreSQL example creates `cdc.public.orders` and `cdc.public.customers` topics and streams row-level changes for those tables.
claims.span_hint: section: Example: CDC with Debezium, concluding sentence
claims.rationale: Debezium's official connector docs are the right primary source for topic naming and change-event semantics, but the domain is off-list.

claims.claim_text: Strimzi can generate its own CA and issue certificates for Kafka components and clients.
claims.span_hint: section: Strimzi TLS: Automatic Certificate Management, intro sentence
claims.rationale: This is a Strimzi security feature claim and the official configuration docs are off-list.

claims.claim_text: Strimzi stores generated certificates in Kubernetes Secrets and supports automated certificate rotation.
claims.span_hint: section: Strimzi TLS: Automatic Certificate Management, closing sentence
claims.rationale: The authoritative source for generated secret formats and rotation behavior is the official Strimzi documentation, which is off-list.

claims.claim_text: For SCRAM-authenticated users, Strimzi creates a Secret containing `password` and `sasl.jaas.config`.
claims.span_hint: section: Creating Authenticated Users with ACLs, credential bullets
claims.rationale: The Strimzi user-authentication docs directly cover generated SCRAM secret contents, but the domain is not allowlisted.

claims.claim_text: For TLS-authenticated users, Strimzi creates a Secret containing client certificate material such as `user.crt`, `user.key`, and `ca.crt`.
claims.span_hint: section: Creating Authenticated Users with ACLs, credential bullets
claims.rationale: This claim depends on Strimzi's certificate-generation documentation, which is off-list.

claims.claim_text: Jay Kreps said, 'Kafka is like a central nervous system for data. Every event that happens in your business flows through it.'
claims.span_hint: epigraph at end of module
claims.anchor_text: *"Kafka is like a central nervous system for data. Every event that happens in your business flows through it."* — Jay Kreps, creator of Apache Kafka
claims.suggested_rewrite: Kafka often acts as a central event backbone for data-intensive systems.
claims.rationale: This is a verbatim attributed quote and no interview, talk, or primary source was established for it.

further_reading.title: Apache Kafka KRaft Operations
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the core upstream reference for controller roles, metadata quorum, and KRaft operational behavior.

further_reading.title: Strimzi Kafka Operator
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the canonical upstream project entrypoint for Strimzi and its Kubernetes-focused Kafka lifecycle tooling.

further_reading.title: Kafka Connect Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: This covers Kafka Connect's role in integrating Kafka with external systems, which is central to the second half of the module.

notes: Per pipeline rules, code blocks, tables, quiz prompts/answers, Mermaid diagrams, and repeated summary restatements were not expanded into separate claims. Several Strimzi and Debezium assertions have good primary sources, but those sources are currently off-allowlist and therefore marked as `needs_allowlist_expansion`.
